title: Neural networks with trainable matrix activation functions

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The training process of neural networks usually optimizes weights and bias parameters of linear transformations, while nonlinear activation functions are prespecified and fixed. This work develops a systematic approach to constructing matrix activation functions whose entries are generalized from ReLU. The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons. The proposed activation functions depend on parameters that are trained along with the weights and bias vectors. Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In recent decades, deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved significant successes in many fields such as computer vision and natural language processing (Voulodimos et al., 2018), (Otter et al., 2018). The DNN surrogate model is constructed using recursive composition of linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions. To achieve good performance, it is essential to choose activation functions suitable for specific applications. In practice, Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is one of the most popular activation functions for its simplicity and efficiency. A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019). Several relatively new activation approaches are proposed to overcome this problem, e.g., the simple Leaky ReLU, and Piecewise Linear Unit (PLU) (Nicolae, 2018), Softplus (Glorot et al., 2011), Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) (Clevert et al., 2016), Scaled Exponential Linear Unit (SELU) (Klambauer et al., 2017), Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2016).

Although the aforementioned activation functions are shown to be competitive in benchmark tests, they are still fixed nonlinear functions. In a DNN structure, it is often hard to determine a priori the optimal activation function for a specific application. In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using function approximation examples and well-known benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. There are a few classical works on adaptively tuning of parameters in the training process, e.g., the parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b). However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.

## REMARK

_The activation functions used in TMAF neural network are not piecewise constants. Instead, TMAF activation is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants. There exist several trainable activation functions in the literature, e.g., Parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b), Pad´e Activation Unit (PAU) (Molina et al., 2020), Piecewise Linear Unit (PWLU) (Zhou et al., 2021), Swish (Ramachandran et al., 2017), adaptive rational functions (Boull´e et al., 2020), adaptive splines (Bohra et al., 2020), adaptive activation functions for physics informed neural networks (ada, 2020). We point out that TMAF is different from the aforementioned activation approaches. One might think TMAF is similar to PWLU because they are both defined piecewise. However, by construction TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a_ continuous _piecewise linear function with trainable slopes._

Starting from the diagonal activation _Dℓ_ , we can go one step further to construct more general activation matrices. First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where

There seems to be no special reason, aside from keeping the computational cost as low as possible, to consider only diagonal operators _D_ . A more ambitious approach is to consider a trainable nonlinear activation _operator_ determined by more general matrices, for example, by a tri-diagonal operator

for _x ∈_ R _[d] ._ The diagonal entry _αℓ_ is given in equation 6 while the off-diagonal entries _βℓ_ , _γℓ_ are piecewise constant functions defined in a fashion similar to _αℓ_ . Theoretically speaking, even trainable full matrix activation is possible despite of potentially huge training cost. In summary, a DNN based on trainable nonlinear activation operators _{Tℓ}[L] ℓ_ =1[reads]

As we indicated above, TMAF can be used with any matrix and this will lead to a great flexibility in training and approximation. In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient. We can choose p-diagonal matrices, as well as matrices with prescribed sparsity patterns. Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.

We now test TMAF by classifying images in the MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 dataset.

diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are shown in Figures 7a, 7b and Table 3. We observe that performance of the TMAF and the ReLU networks are similar. Such a behavior clearly should be expected for a simple dataset such as MNIST.

For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 8a, 8b and Table 3. Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU. Nevertheless, TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.

For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.

It is possible to improve the performance of TMAF applied to those benchmark datasets. The key point is to select suitable intervals in _αℓ_ to optimize the performance. A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a novel framework for trainable matrix activation functions (TMAF), generalizing ReLU through piecewise constant diagonal and tri-diagonal matrix operators. It demonstrates improved performance on function approximation and image classification tasks relative to ReLU and parametric ReLU. The core idea—extending scalar trainable activations to matrix forms with structured sparsity—is conceptually interesting and aligns with recent trends in adaptive neural components. The numerical results suggest potential gains, particularly in capturing oscillatory functions where standard activations struggle.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation studies isolating TMAF's contribution to performance: The paper claims improved accuracy with TMAF but does not isolate the source of gains. For instance, in the oscillatory function task, TMAF uses 400 neurons per layer while ReLU networks use the same architecture—but no comparison is made to a ReLU network with increased width or depth that might achieve similar performance. The argument that TMAF requires fewer parameters than deeper ReLU nets is speculative without direct comparison. Without ablation studies varying the number of intervals, matrix structure (diagonal vs. tri-diagonal), or parameter count, it is impossible to attribute gains to the proposed mechanism rather than increased model capacity or tuning. This undermines the claim that matrix activation structure inherently improves expressivity.

- Insufficient comparison with modern activation functions: The evaluation compares TMAF only against ReLU and parametric ReLU, ignoring widely adopted alternatives like Swish, Mish, or GELU, which are known to outperform ReLU on vision tasks. Given that these functions are smooth, learnable-like, and often more expressive, their omission is a critical gap. The paper positions TMAF as a competitive alternative, but without benchmarking against these baselines—especially in ResNet architectures where Swish is known to help—the significance of the reported CIFAR-10/100 gains (77.5% vs. 80.2%) remains unclear. This weakens the empirical contribution and limits practical relevance.

- Overparameterization risk not addressed: TMAF introduces a large number of trainable parameters through piecewise constant functions with many intervals (e.g., 100+ intervals in Section 3.2). For a layer with 400 neurons and 100 interval thresholds, this adds 40,000 scalar parameters per layer just for the activation—comparable to or exceeding the weight matrix size. The paper does not report parameter counts, FLOPs, or inference speed, making it impossible to assess computational efficiency. Given that modern activations like Swish add only one scalar per channel, the cost-benefit trade-off of TMAF is unexamined. This raises concerns about scalability and overfitting, especially on small datasets like MNIST where gains are minimal.

- Training dynamics and stability not analyzed: The paper assumes stable training with ADAM but provides no evidence that the matrix activation parameters converge reliably or do not induce gradient instability. With piecewise constant functions involving thresholds and discrete jumps, gradients may vanish or become noisy during backpropagation. The lack of learning curves for activation parameters, gradient norms, or sensitivity to initialization leaves open the question of whether TMAF is robust in practice. This is a notable omission given the focus on 'robustness' in the abstract and the known challenges with non-smooth trainable activations.

- Generalization claims rest on narrow benchmarks: Performance is evaluated only on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100—moderately sized datasets with well-known limitations in assessing architectural novelty. No tests are conducted on larger-scale or more complex data (e.g., ImageNet, COCO, or language tasks), nor on out-of-distribution robustness, transfer learning, or real-world applications. The claim that TMAF is 'robust' and broadly applicable is therefore overstated. Given that even minor architectural changes can yield spurious gains on CIFAR, broader validation is needed to support generalization claims.

- Theoretical motivation is weak and inconsistent: The paper frames TMAF as a generalization of ReLU via matrix multiplication, but the connection is largely algebraic rather than functional. The derivation in Section 2 shows that ReLU can be written as a diagonal matrix operation, but this is a reformulation, not an extension. The step from there to arbitrary piecewise constant matrices lacks theoretical justification—why should such matrices improve approximation? No expressivity analysis, universal approximation guarantee, or convergence theory is provided. Moreover, the remark that 'TMAF includes discontinuous functions' suggests a loss of smoothness, which may hurt optimization, yet this is not discussed.

- No ablation on interval count or matrix structure: The paper attributes performance gains to the matrix activation framework but does not isolate which components matter. For instance, in the oscillatory function task, diagonal TMAF uses 100 intervals—does reducing this to 10 or 5 degrade performance significantly? Similarly, tri-diagonal TMAF slightly outperforms diagonal, but is this due to connectivity or simply more parameters? The paper must include ablation studies varying the number of intervals and matrix sparsity pattern while controlling for total parameter count. Compute the test error for diagonal TMAF on the oscillatory function with 5, 20, and 100 intervals, using the same architecture and parameter budget, and report the trade-off between flexibility and performance.

- Missing comparison with Swish, GELU, and other modern activations: The paper claims TMAF is competitive and more robust than ReLU but omits comparisons with standard learnable or smooth alternatives. In ResNet18 on CIFAR-10, Swish and GELU typically outperform ReLU by 1–2 percentage points; without showing TMAF vs. these baselines under the same training setup, the 2.7% gain over ReLU could simply reflect the benefit of adaptive nonlinearity rather than the matrix structure. The authors should retrain ResNet18 with Swish, GELU, and TMAF using identical hyperparameters and initialization, then report accuracy and convergence speed. This is necessary to support the claim of competitiveness.

- No computational cost analysis: TMAF introduces potentially thousands of additional trainable parameters per layer through piecewise constant functions, yet the paper reports no parameter counts, FLOPs, or inference times. For ResNet18 on CIFAR-10, compute the total number of parameters and measure the per-epoch training time for both ReLU and TMAF variants. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the accuracy gain is worth the cost. Given that efficient activation functions like Swish add only one scalar per channel, the lack of efficiency metrics undermines claims of practical relevance.

- Training dynamics of activation parameters unreported: The paper assumes stable training of matrix activation parameters but provides no evidence. For the CIFAR-10 experiment with TMAF, plot the evolution of a few representative interval values $t_{\ell,j}$ and off-diagonal coefficients $\beta_{\ell}, \gamma_{\ell}$ during training. Are they converging? Do they vary across layers? Without such analysis, it is unclear whether the activation parameters are learning meaningful patterns or simply overfitting noise. Given the non-smoothness of piecewise constant functions, gradient behavior is a legitimate concern that must be addressed.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add ablation studies controlling for model capacity, including comparisons with wider/deeper ReLU networks and analysis of parameter efficiency.

- Include comparisons with modern activation functions (Swish, Mish, GELU) on all benchmarks.

- Report computational cost (parameter count, FLOPs, training/inference time) and analyze overparameterization risks.

- Provide analysis of training dynamics: convergence of activation parameters, gradient behavior, and sensitivity to initialization.

- Extend evaluation to at least one larger-scale dataset (e.g., ImageNet) or downstream task to support generalization claims.

comments.title: Incorrect functional composition notation
comments.quote: Having a closer look at ReLU $\sigma$, we observe that the activation $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}(x)=\sigma(\eta_{\ell}(x))$ could be realized as a matrix-vector multiplication $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}(x)=D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x))\eta_{\ell}(x)$ or equivalently $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}=(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$, where $\eta_{\ell}$ is column vector-valued in $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$ and $D_{\ell}$ is a diagonal matrix-valued function mapping from $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}\times n_{\ell}}$ with entries from the discrete set $\{0,1\}$.
comments.explanation: The expression $(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$ incorrectly combines function composition and matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is not a function composition but a pointwise operator involving both composition and multiplication, so equating it to $\sigma \circ \eta_{\ell}$ is type-incorrect. The intended identity is $\sigma(\eta_{\ell}(x)) = D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x)) \eta_{\ell}(x)$, where $D_{\ell}(y)$ is diagonal with entries $1$ if $y_i > 0$ and $0$ otherwise. Rewrite the statement to avoid the misleading functional equality and define the operation explicitly as a matrix-vector product dependent on the input.

comments.title: Unjustified equivalence for non-diagonal matrices
comments.quote: For the time being, let $D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x))\eta_{\ell}(x)=\sigma_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x))$ with $\sigma_{\ell}$ being a scalar-valued function applied to $\eta_{\ell}(x)$ component-wise.
comments.explanation: This equation asserts that a matrix-vector product can be written as a component-wise scalar function, which only holds if $D_{\ell}$ is diagonal. For non-diagonal matrices, output components depend on multiple inputs—e.g., $[Dy]_1 = d_{11}y_1 + d_{12}y_2$ cannot be expressed as $\sigma_{\ell}(y_1)$. The paper does not restrict this equality to diagonal $D_{\ell}$, creating a logical error in the general case. Add a clear qualification that this equivalence applies only when $D_{\ell}$ is diagonal, and revise the surrounding discussion to reflect that non-diagonal operators introduce inter-neuron dependencies.

comments.title: Ambiguity in recurrence relation due to operator precedence
comments.quote: $\eta_1 = h_{W_1,b_1},\quad \eta_{\ell+1} = h_{W_{\ell+1},b_{\ell+1}} \circ \big{[}(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}\big{]},\quad \ell=1,\ldots,L-1.$
comments.explanation: The expression $h_{W_{\ell+1},b_{\ell+1}} \circ \big{[}(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}\big{]}$ is ill-typed: $h_{W,b}$ is a function, but $(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$ is a vector-valued expression, not a function, so composition is undefined. The intended meaning is pointwise application: $\eta_{\ell+1}(x) = h_{W_{\ell+1},b_{\ell+1}}\left( D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x)) \eta_{\ell}(x) \right)$. Rewrite the recurrence using explicit evaluation to eliminate type mismatches and clarify the computational flow.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy formula indexing
comments.quote:
The cross entropy loss function of $\eta_{L,\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}}$ then is defined by

$\mathcal{E}(\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}) = -\frac{1}{p} \sum_{k=1}^p \log\left(\frac{\exp(x_{c_k,k})}{\sum_{j=1}^M \exp(x_{j,k})}\right).$
comments.explanation: The notation $x_{j,k}$ is undefined and inconsistent. The network output $x_k = \eta(z_k)$ is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^M$, so $x_{j,k}$ suggests a matrix layout that was never introduced. The correct form should use $(x_k)_j$ to denote the $j$-th component of the $k$-th sample's output. Rewrite the loss as $\mathcal{E} = -\frac{1}{p} \sum_{k=1}^p \log\left(\frac{\exp((x_k)_{c_k})}{\sum_{j=1}^M \exp((x_k)_j)}\right)$ to reflect proper indexing and avoid ambiguity in the logit structure.

comments.title: Incorrect threshold values in Figure 1(b)
comments.explanation: The threshold values $t_{\ell,0} = 0$, $t_{\ell,1} = 1$, $t_{\ell,2} = 0$ violate monotonicity, as $t_{\ell,2} < t_{\ell,1}$. For $m=2$, the interval boundaries must satisfy $s_1 < s_2$, but the values imply overlapping or undefined intervals. This suggests either a typo or a fundamental error in defining the piecewise function. Revise the thresholds to an increasing sequence—e.g., $t_{\ell,2} = 2$—to ensure a valid partition of the domain.

comments.title: Incorrect interval probability under Gaussian
comments.quote: For TMAF $D_{\ell}$ in equation 5, the function $\alpha_{\ell}$ uses intervals $(-\infty,-1.4)$, $(-1.4,-0.92]$, $(-0.92,-0.56]$, $(-0.56,-0.26]$, $(-0.26,0]$, $(0,0.26]$, $(0.26,0.56]$, $(0.56,0.92]$, $(0.92,1.4]$, $(1.4,\infty)$ such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The claim that each interval has 0.1 probability under a standard Gaussian is false. For example, $\Phi(-1.4) \approx 0.0808$, so $(-\infty, -1.4)$ has probability ~8.1%, not 10%. The actual deciles of the standard normal are at $\Phi^{-1}(k/10)$, e.g., $\Phi^{-1}(0.1) \approx -1.28$. The current breakpoints do not yield equiprobable bins. Either correct the values to match Gaussian quantiles or remove the claim of equal probability if the intervals were chosen heuristically.

comments.title: Input distribution mismatch invalidates interval rationale
comments.quote: The training datasets consist of 20000 input-output data pairs where the input data are randomly sampled from the hypercube $[-2,2]^{n}$ based on uniform distribution. The neural networks have single or double hidden layers. Each layer (except input and output layers) has $20$ neurons. For TMAF $D_{\ell}$ in equation 5, the function $\alpha_{\ell}$ uses intervals $(-\infty,-1.4)$, $(-1.4,-0.92]$, $(-0.92,-0.56]$, $(-0.56,-0.26]$, $(-0.26,0]$, $(0,0.26]$, $(0.26,0.56]$, $(0.56,0.92]$, $(0.92,1.4]$, $(1.4,\infty)$ such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The input is uniformly distributed, but the activation intervals are designed for Gaussian pre-activations. However, $Wx + b$ with $x \sim \text{Uniform}([-2,2]^n)$ does not generally yield Gaussian-distributed pre-activations unless $n$ is large and weights are scaled appropriately. The paper provides no justification for assuming Gaussianity in the first layer. Either justify the Gaussian approximation via central limit theorem and initialization, or base interval selection on the empirical distribution of pre-activations.

comments.title: BatchNorm invalidates fixed interval design
comments.quote: Moreover, we apply BatchNorm1d in PyTorch to the linear output of neural networks in each hidden layer.
comments.explanation: Batch normalization standardizes pre-activations to zero mean and unit variance, making their distribution approximately standard normal across batches. This dynamic rescaling renders fixed thresholds—designed based on static Gaussian quantiles—arbitrary unless they are redefined relative to the normalized scale. Yet the paper uses fixed intervals without adaptation. Either make the thresholds learnable, justify their fixed nature post-BatchNorm, or remove BatchNorm to preserve the validity of the interval design.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of tri-diagonal operator in Equation (7)
comments.quote: The diagonal entry  $\alpha_{\ell}$  is given in equation 6 while the off-diagonal entries  $\beta_{\ell}, \gamma_{\ell}$  are piecewise constant functions defined in a fashion similar to  $\alpha_{\ell}$ .
comments.explanation: The phrase 'defined in a fashion similar to $\alpha_{\ell}$' is mathematically vague. It is unclear whether $\beta_{\ell}(g_j(x))$ depends on $g_j(x)$ or is shared across neurons, and how thresholds are assigned. This ambiguity affects gradient computation and model reproducibility. Define explicitly how $\beta_{\ell}$ and $\gamma_{\ell}$ depend on the input—e.g., $\beta_{\ell}(g_k(x))$ for specific $k$—and specify whether these functions are per-neuron or shared.

comments.title: Failure to define training dynamics for step function parameters
comments.quote: In our case, we will choose the grid points $\{s_{j}\}_{j=1}^{m}$ a priori and train the step values $\cup_{\ell=1}^{L}\{t_{\ell,j}\}_{j=0}^{m}$ of $\alpha_{\ell}$.
comments.explanation: The step function $\alpha_{\ell}(s)$ is piecewise constant, so its derivative is zero almost everywhere, and standard backpropagation cannot update $t_{\ell,j}$. The paper does not mention any gradient approximation method (e.g., straight-through estimator, smoothing). Without such a mechanism, training is infeasible. Add a description of the gradient estimation technique used, or reformulate $\alpha_{\ell}$ as a differentiable surrogate.

comments.title: Misleading claim about computational efficiency of sparse matrices
comments.quote: In fact, if a sparse matrix  $D$  is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: The cost of sparse matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of non-zero entries, not the number of rows. A sparse matrix with $n$ rows and $O(n^2)$ non-zeros (e.g., wide banded matrix) still requires $O(n^2)$ operations. The statement misrepresents computational complexity. Revise to clarify that efficiency depends on sparsity density, and specify that for fixed-bandwidth p-diagonal matrices, cost is $O(n)$ per layer.

comments.title: Incorrect input dimension for MNIST
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer  $n_0 = 764$ ), and we use ReLU or
comments.explanation: The input dimension $n_0 = 764$ is incorrect. MNIST images are $28 \times 28$, so the flattened input size is 784. This discrepancy suggests a typo or unexplained preprocessing. Since the rest of the paper refers to standard benchmarks, this error undermines reproducibility. Correct the value to $n_0 = 784$.

comments.title: Confusion between model depth and layer definition
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer  $n_0 = 764$ ), and we use ReLU or
comments.explanation: The term 'double layer' is ambiguous and nonstandard. If the network has two hidden layers, it should be described as such. Given that each has only 10 neurons, the architecture is unusually narrow for MNIST. Clarify the intended depth by rewriting as 'a fully connected network with two hidden layers of 10 neurons each' to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of ReLU's gradient issue
comments.quote: A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem *(Lu et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: The dying ReLU problem refers to neurons becoming inactive and outputting zero, leading to zero gradients and no updates—this is distinct from classical vanishing gradients due to multiplicative decay in deep networks. ReLU actually mitigates traditional vanishing gradients because its gradient is 1 for positive inputs. The conflation weakens the motivation. Revise to clarify that ReLU avoids vanishing gradients but suffers from neuron death when activations get stuck at zero.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Trainable Matrix Activation Functions (TMAF) for neural networks, generalizing ReLU by using diagonal matrices with trainable piecewise constant entries. While the concept is interesting and the numerical experiments show promise, the paper lacks rigorous theoretical analysis, comprehensive benchmarking, and computational efficiency evaluation needed for a top venue.

The paper presents a novel approach to trainable activations using matrix-valued functions, with promising results on function approximation tasks and modest improvements on CIFAR datasets. The methodology is clearly explained and the implementation details are sufficient for reproduction. However, the paper falls short on theoretical justification, scalability analysis, and thorough comparison with state-of-the-art methods.

Overview issues:

- Missing computational complexity analysis: The paper claims TMAF networks are "simple and efficient" but provides no analysis of computational overhead. For diagonal TMAF with m intervals, each activation requires O(m) comparisons and multiplications per neuron, plus storing m parameters per neuron. For deep networks with thousands of neurons, this could significantly increase memory usage and training time. The paper should include FLOP counts, memory requirements, and training time comparisons against ReLU baselines, especially for the tri-diagonal case which adds O(n) additional parameters per layer.

- Insufficient benchmark comparison: The experiments only compare against ReLU and Parametric ReLU, ignoring recent state-of-the-art trainable activations like Swish, GELU, PAU, and PWLU mentioned in the literature review. For CIFAR-10, the reported 80.2% accuracy with TMAF is below modern benchmarks (ResNet18 achieves >94% with standard techniques). Without comparisons to contemporary methods on the same architectures, the claimed improvements cannot be properly evaluated. The paper should include at least Swish/GELU and one recent trainable activation method.

- No gradient stability analysis: The piecewise constant activation functions in TMAF have zero derivative almost everywhere, which raises concerns about gradient flow during backpropagation. While the paper mentions avoiding the "dying ReLU problem," it provides no theoretical or empirical analysis of gradient propagation. The discontinuous case shown in Figure 1b could cause training instability. The paper should analyze the Jacobian of the activation, discuss gradient flow properties, and report gradient norms during training to demonstrate stability.

- Limited to simple datasets: The classification experiments are limited to MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 using standard architectures. These datasets are insufficient to demonstrate the practical value of TMAF for modern deep learning tasks. The literature context warns that improvements on MNIST/CIFAR may not translate to more complex datasets. The paper should test TMAF on at least one challenging dataset like ImageNet or a domain-specific task where the additional parameterization could provide meaningful benefits.

- Lack of theoretical justification: The paper provides no theoretical analysis of why matrix activations should be more expressive than scalar activations. While the generalization from ReLU to matrix form is mathematically sound, there is no discussion of approximation power, universal approximation theorems, or how the additional parameters contribute to model capacity. A theoretical analysis showing that TMAF can represent functions that scalar activations cannot, or that it achieves better sample complexity, would strengthen the contribution.

- Unclear parameter efficiency claims: The paper claims TMAF is more parameter-efficient than increasing network width, but provides no comparison. For m=10 intervals and n neurons per layer, TMAF adds 10n parameters per layer versus n² for adding n neurons. This comparison is never quantified. The paper should explicitly analyze the parameter trade-off between using TMAF versus increasing network depth/width, and demonstrate scenarios where TMAF provides better performance per parameter.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous improvement: The paper claims TMAF is more robust than parametric ReLU but provides no concrete example where parametric ReLU fails while TMAF succeeds. For a methodological contribution like this, readers need to see a clear case where the additional matrix parameters solve a problem that scalar activations cannot. The oscillatory function example comes close but doesn't directly compare with parametric ReLU on the same architecture. The authors should add a specific function approximation task where parametric ReLU gets stuck in local minima or cannot achieve target accuracy, while TMAF converges successfully. This would demonstrate that the matrix structure provides genuine benefits beyond just having more parameters.

- No worked computation for standard model: The paper presents the TMAF framework but never computes the activation matrices for a concrete, fully-specified neural network. Readers would benefit from seeing the exact form of D_ℓ for a simple network like a 2-layer MLP with 4 neurons, including the trained parameter values {t_ℓ,j} after training on a toy problem. This worked example would clarify how the matrix activation differs from scalar activation in practice and help readers implement the method. The current mathematical presentation remains abstract, making it difficult to gauge the actual learned activation patterns.

- Missing inference discussion for matrix parameters: The paper introduces many trainable parameters in the activation matrices but provides no guidance on how to estimate them efficiently in practice. For deep networks, the number of activation parameters scales with O(mn) per layer, which could lead to overfitting or slow convergence. The paper should discuss initialization strategies for {t_ℓ,j}, regularization approaches, and whether the activation parameters need different learning rates than weights. Without this implementation guidance, practitioners cannot reproduce the results effectively, especially for the tri-diagonal case which introduces even more parameters.

- Underdeveloped robustness implications: The abstract and introduction claim TMAF is "more robust" but this is never precisely defined or demonstrated. The paper shows better approximation accuracy on oscillatory functions but doesn't test robustness to common challenges like noisy inputs, adversarial examples, or distribution shift. For a method claiming robustness advantages, the authors should include experiments testing sensitivity to input perturbations or out-of-distribution data, comparing degradation rates against baseline activations. This would substantiate the robustness claim beyond just approximation accuracy.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive computational complexity analysis including FLOPs, memory usage, and training time comparisons

- Include comparisons with modern activation functions (Swish, GELU, PAU) on the same architectures

- Provide gradient stability analysis with theoretical justification and empirical gradient norm tracking

- Test on at least one large-scale dataset beyond CIFAR to demonstrate practical scalability

- Add theoretical analysis of approximation power and expressiveness compared to scalar activations

comments.title: Incorrect characterization of ReLU gradient issue
comments.quote: A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem *(Lu et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: The dying ReLU problem is not caused by vanishing gradients but by neurons becoming permanently inactive (outputting zero) due to negative inputs, which results in zero gradients for those neurons during backpropagation. The gradient of ReLU is either 0 (for negative inputs) or 1 (for positive inputs), so it doesn't vanish in the traditional sense of becoming arbitrarily small. Rewrite this sentence as: "A drawback of ReLU is that neurons can become permanently inactive during training, known as the dying ReLU problem, where negative inputs result in zero gradients that prevent weight updates."

comments.title: Mathematically incorrect matrix-vector notation
comments.quote: Having a closer look at ReLU $\sigma$, we observe that the activation $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}(x)=\sigma(\eta_{\ell}(x))$ could be realized as a matrix-vector multiplication $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}(x)=D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x))\eta_{\ell}(x)$ or equivalently $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}=(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$, where $\eta_{\ell}$ is column vector-valued in $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$ and $D_{\ell}$ is a diagonal matrix-valued function mapping from $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}\times n_{\ell}}$ with entries from the discrete set $\{0,1\}$.
comments.explanation: The equivalence $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}=(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$ is mathematically incorrect. The left side $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}$ is a function from $\mathbb{R}^{d_0}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$, while the right side $(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$ is ambiguous notation. The expression $(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})$ would be a matrix-valued function, but multiplying it by $\eta_{\ell}$ without evaluating at a point is not well-defined. The correct formulation should be $\sigma\circ\eta_{\ell}(x) = (D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})(x) \cdot \eta_{\ell}(x)$ for all $x$, where $(D_{\ell}\circ\eta_{\ell})(x) = D_{\ell}(\eta_{\ell}(x))$.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss indexing error
comments.quote:
ross entropy loss function of $\eta_{L,\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}}$ then is defined by

$\mathcal{E}(\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}) = -\frac{1}{p} \sum_{k=1}^p \log\left(\frac{\exp(x_{c_k,k})}{\sum_{j=1}^M \exp(x_{j,k})}\right).$

### 3.1 A
comments.explanation: The cross-entropy loss function has an indexing error in the denominator. The denominator sums over $j=1$ to $M$ of $\exp(x_{j,k})$, but $x_{j,k}$ is undefined - the network output for sample $k$ should be denoted as $x_k$ with components $x_{k,j}$. The correct formula should be $\mathcal{E}(\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}) = -\frac{1}{p} \sum_{k=1}^p \log\left(\frac{\exp(x_{k,c_k})}{\sum_{j=1}^M \exp(x_{k,j})}\right)$. This error makes the mathematical formulation ambiguous and could lead to implementation errors.

comments.title: Unjustified performance superiority claim
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: This logical inference is invalid. The fact that TMAF includes ReLU and Leaky ReLU as special cases does not guarantee superior or even equal performance in practice. The additional trainable parameters $\{t_{\ell,j}\}$ could lead to overfitting, slower convergence, or poor local minima. The optimization landscape becomes more complex with $m+1$ parameters per neuron per layer versus 0 for ReLU. Without theoretical analysis of generalization or empirical evidence showing the optimization can find better configurations than the ReLU/Leaky ReLU special cases, this claim is unsupported.

comments.title: Distribution inconsistency in interval selection
comments.quote: neurons. For TMAF $D_{\ell}$ in equation 5, the function $\alpha_{\ell}$ uses intervals $(-\infty,-1.4)$, $(-1.4,-0.92]$, $(-0.92,-0.56]$, $(-0.56,-0.26]$, $(-0.26,0]$, $(0,0.26]$, $(0.26,0.56]$, $(0.56,0.92]$, $(0.92,1.4]$, $(1.4,\infty)$ such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The intervals are designed to have equal probability (0.1) under a Gaussian distribution, but training data is uniformly sampled from [-2,2]^n. Under uniform distribution, the outer intervals (-∞,-1.4] and (1.4,∞) each contain (2-1.4)/4 = 0.15 of the data, not 0.1. This creates a systematic bias where the activation function will process significantly more data through the outer intervals than intended. The interval selection criterion should match the uniform distribution used for training data.

comments.title: BatchNorm1d application unclear
comments.quote: Moreover, we apply BatchNorm1d in PyTorch to the linear output of neural networks in each hidden layer.
comments.explanation: The text states BatchNorm1d is applied to "the linear output of neural networks in each hidden layer" but doesn't specify whether this occurs before or after the TMAF activation. This ordering is crucial as BatchNorm changes the distribution that TMAF's intervals are designed for. If BatchNorm is applied before TMAF, the Gaussian-based interval design becomes even more inappropriate since BatchNorm outputs approximately N(0,1). Clarify the order of BatchNorm1d and TMAF activation in the network architecture.

comments.title: MNIST input dimension error
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer  $n_0 = 764$ ), and we use ReLU or
comments.explanation: The paper states the first layer has $n_0 = 764$ neurons, but MNIST images are 28×28 pixels, which should be 784 input features (28×28 = 784), not 764. This appears to be a typographical error in the input dimension specification. Since the experiments are described as using standard MNIST, this dimension mismatch needs correction.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in recurrence relation
comments.quote: $\eta_1 = h_{W_1,b_1},\quad \eta_{\ell+1} = h_{W_{\ell+1},b_{\ell+1}} \circ \big{[}(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}\big{]},\quad \ell=1,\ldots,L-1.$
comments.explanation: The notation $(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})\eta_{\ell}$ in the recurrence relation is ambiguous and mathematically imprecise. As noted in comment 2, this should be written as $(D_{\ell} \circ \eta_{\ell})(x) \cdot \eta_{\ell}(x)$ for all $x$. The current notation could be misinterpreted as multiplying a matrix-valued function by a vector without evaluation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency claim
comments.quote: Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the approach is "efficient" without providing any computational analysis. Matrix activation functions introduce additional parameters and matrix-vector multiplications compared to scalar activations like ReLU. Each activation now requires O(n²) operations for an n×n matrix versus O(n) for scalar activations. Without complexity analysis or empirical timing comparisons, this efficiency claim is unsupported.

comments.title: Vague robustness claim
comments.quote: Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.
comments.explanation: The term "robust" is undefined and unsubstantiated. Robustness could refer to gradient stability, noise tolerance, adversarial resilience, or generalization performance. The abstract provides no indication which type of robustness is demonstrated or how it is measured. Without specifying the robustness metric or citing quantitative results, this claim cannot be evaluated.

comments.title: Inconsistent robustness claim without evidence
comments.quote: However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.
comments.explanation: This claim of robustness appears in the introduction but the paper's experiments only show function approximation accuracy on oscillatory functions and classification accuracy on CIFAR datasets. Robustness typically refers to performance under perturbations, noise, or distribution shift, which are not tested. The claim should either be removed or rephrased to match what is actually demonstrated.

comments.title: Unjustified universal approximation claim
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose $\alpha_{\ell}$ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: The statement that piecewise constant functions can approximate continuous functions is true, but it does not justify the specific form of the step function with fixed grid points $\{s_j\}_{j=1}^{m}$ and only trainable values $\{t_{\ell,j}\}_{j=0}^{m}$. For universal approximation, the grid points themselves need to be adaptable to the function being approximated. With fixed grid points, the approximation capability is limited by the choice of these points.

comments.title: Table 1 function mismatch
comments.quote: Table 1: Approximation errors for  $\sin \left( {{\pi }_{x1} + \cdots  + {\pi }_{xn}}\right)$  by neural networks
comments.explanation: The table caption claims to show approximation errors for $\sin(\pi_{x1} + \cdots + \pi_{xn})$, but Figure 2's caption and the surrounding text discuss $\sin(\pi x_1 + \dots + \pi x_n)$. The section's main example approximates $f(x) = \sin(100\pi x) + \cos(50\pi x) + \sin(\pi x)$. This creates ambiguity about which function Table 1 actually reports results for.

comments.title: Missing CIFAR-100 quantitative results
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The paper states that TMAF outperforms ReLU on CIFAR-100 and references Figures 9a and 9b, but these figures are not included in the provided text. More importantly, unlike the MNIST and CIFAR-10 cases which reference Table 3 for specific numerical results, no quantitative results are provided for CIFAR-100. The claim of superior performance without supporting numerical data cannot be verified.

comments.title: Inconsistent network output notation
comments.quote:
The neural network maps $z_{j}$ to $x_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{M}$,

$x_j := \eta_{L,\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}}(z_j) \in \mathbb{R}^M,\quad z_j \in \mathbb{R}^N,\quad j=1,\ldots,p.$
comments.explanation: There's a notation inconsistency between the network output definition and its use in the cross-entropy formula. The network maps $z_j$ to $x_j$, but in the cross-entropy formula, the output is indexed as $x_{c_k,k}$ and $x_{j,k}$. The notation should be consistent - either use $x_j$ with components $x_{j,i}$ throughout, or use $x_{k,j}$ where $k$ indexes samples and $j$ indexes classes.

comments.title: Undefined notation in table caption
comments.quote: Table 1: Approximation errors for  $\sin \left( {{\pi }_{x1} + \cdots  + {\pi }_{xn}}\right)$  by neural networks
comments.explanation: The notation ${{\pi }_{x1} + \cdots  + {\pi }_{xn}}$ in Table 1's caption is mathematically malformed. It appears to intend $\pi x_1 + \cdots + \pi x_n$ but incorrectly places $\pi$ as a subscript of each $x_i$. Rewrite the caption as "Approximation errors for $\sin(\pi x_1 + \cdots + \pi x_n)$ by neural networks" to fix the mathematical notation.

comments.title: Undefined notation in interval definitions
comments.quote: The tri-diagonal TMAF is given in equation 7, where  $\alpha_{\ell}$  is the same as the diagonal TMAF, while  $\beta_{\ell}$  is piecewise constant with respect to intervals  $(- \infty, -2.01 + \underline{h}]$ ,  $\left\{(-2.01 + kh, -2.01 + (k + 1)h]\right\}_{k=0}^{99}$ ,  $(-0.01, \infty)$ , and  $\gamma_{\ell}$  is a piecewise constant based on  $(- \infty, 0.01]$ ,  $\left\{(0.01 + kh, 0.01 + (k + 1)h]\right\}_{k=0}^{99}$ ,  $(2.01, \infty)$ , respectively.
comments.explanation: The notation $\underline{h}$ appears in the interval $(-\infty, -2.01 + \underline{h}]$ but is never defined in the text. Since $h = 0.02$ is defined for the diagonal TMAF, $\underline{h}$ likely refers to the same value, but this should be explicitly stated.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Trainable Matrix Activation Functions (TMAF), which generalize ReLU by using trainable piecewise constant functions within diagonal or sparse matrix operators. While the approach shows empirical promise in function approximation and classification tasks, the work lacks a rigorous analysis of the computational overhead, stability, and the conditions under which these learnable nonlinearities outperform standard fixed activations.

The paper proposes an interesting, flexible framework for learning activation functions that captures complex oscillations better than standard ReLU. Its strength lies in the clear empirical demonstration on high-frequency function approximation. However, the lack of discussion regarding hardware efficiency and the theoretical implications of increasing parameter count in the activation layer limits its impact.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Computational Efficiency Analysis: The paper claims the proposed TMAF is 'simple and efficient' (Section 1), yet it introduces matrix-vector multiplications for activation layers that are significantly more complex than the element-wise operations used by ReLU, Swish, or GELU. Standard hardware accelerators like GPUs are heavily optimized for element-wise nonlinearities. The paper fails to provide any benchmarks on memory footprint, training time per epoch, or hardware utilization compared to standard baselines. Without this, the claim of efficiency is unsubstantiated, as the overhead of matrix operations could easily negate the benefits of fewer neurons.

- Insufficient Justification for Initialization: The training of piecewise constant functions (Equation 6) is notoriously sensitive to initialization, especially when the function is discontinuous. The paper mentions that grid points are fixed but does not explain how the initial values of the parameters {t_{l,j}} are chosen. If these are initialized to zero or random values, the gradient flow in the early stages of training may be severely compromised, leading to the 'dying' behavior the authors aim to avoid. A detailed discussion on initialization strategy is necessary to ensure the reproducibility of the results.

- Conflation of Expressivity with Model Depth: The authors suggest that TMAF provides a unique advantage in representational power, but they do not address the concern that many trainable activations are effectively equivalent to adding additional layers with fixed activations and specific constraints. In Equation 4, the matrix-vector multiplication is a learnable operation that could be interpreted as a hidden layer with a specific sparsity pattern. The paper needs to perform an ablation study comparing a TMAF network with a standard ReLU network that has an equivalent number of total parameters to determine if the gain comes from the activation function itself or simply from the increased capacity.

- Risk of Overfitting on Small Datasets: The flexibility of the TMAF approach, particularly the tri-diagonal version in Section 2, introduces a large number of parameters into the activation layer. While the paper demonstrates improvements on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these datasets are relatively small compared to modern benchmarks. There is a significant risk that these activation functions are overfitting the training distribution rather than learning a generalizable nonlinearity. The authors should include a study on the sensitivity of the model to the number of intervals (m) and provide evidence that the model does not overfit as the flexibility of the activation increases.

- Lack of gradient flow analysis for discontinuous activation functions: The paper introduces discontinuous activation functions as a special case of the TMAF framework. Discontinuities in the activation function can lead to zero or undefined gradients during backpropagation, which directly contradicts the goal of solving the dying ReLU problem. The authors must provide an analysis of the gradient flow through these discontinuities or describe the specific sub-gradient implementation used to ensure training stability. A simple demonstration showing the gradient magnitude across the threshold s_j in a toy network would suffice to prove that the training process does not stall.

- Absence of parameter-matched baseline comparisons: The paper claims that TMAF increases representational power, but it adds learnable parameters to the activation layer. To isolate the benefit of the matrix activation itself, the authors should compare their model against a standard ReLU network that has an equivalent total parameter count, achieved by increasing the width of the hidden layers. Without this comparison, it is impossible to determine if the performance gains are due to the TMAF architecture or simply the increased capacity of the model. A table showing the performance of a Wide ReLU network alongside the current results would clarify this contribution.

- Missing sensitivity analysis of grid point selection: The performance of the TMAF depends on the choice of grid points {s_j} for the piecewise constant function alpha_l. The paper currently fixes these points a priori, but provides no guidance on how to choose them for a new dataset or task. The authors should conduct an ablation study on a representative task, such as the oscillatory function approximation, to show how the final loss varies with different grid point densities or distributions. This is necessary to demonstrate that the approach is not overly sensitive to the specific initialization of the interval grid.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include a comprehensive computational benchmark comparing training time and memory usage of TMAF against standard ReLU/GELU networks on modern hardware.

- Perform an ablation study comparing TMAF against a baseline network with an equivalent number of parameters to isolate the benefit of the learnable activation.

- Provide a detailed analysis of the initialization strategy for the piecewise constant parameters {t_{l,j}} and its impact on convergence stability.

- Conduct a sensitivity analysis regarding the number of intervals (m) to demonstrate that the increased flexibility does not lead to overfitting.

comments.title: Inconsistency between reported loss and qualitative performance claims
comments.quote: For this challenging problem, we note that the diagonal TMAF and tri-diagonal TMAF produce high-quality approximations (see Figures 5 and 4b) while ReLU and parametric ReLU are not able to approximate the highly oscillating function within reasonable accuracy, see Figure 4b and Table 2.
comments.explanation: The reported loss of 1.00 for ReLU and Para ReLU in Table 2 is inconsistent with the claim that these models capture the low-frequency component of the target function. A loss of 1.00 is characteristic of a model predicting a constant value of zero, which suggests the model is failing to capture even the low-frequency components. Please clarify whether the loss is normalized or revise the text to acknowledge that ReLU-type networks fail to capture any oscillatory components.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of ReLU vanishing gradient problem
comments.quote: awback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem *(Lu et al., 2019)*. Se
comments.explanation: The dying ReLU problem and the vanishing gradient problem are distinct phenomena. The dying ReLU problem refers to neurons becoming inactive and outputting zero, while the vanishing gradient problem typically refers to the exponential decay of gradients in deep networks due to saturation. ReLU is generally used to mitigate the vanishing gradient problem, not cause it. Please distinguish between these two issues in the text.

comments.title: Unspecified continuity requirements for the approximation
comments.quote: piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose $\alpha_{\ell}$ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: The use of step functions introduces discontinuities in the activation function unless specific conditions are met at the jump points. The paper does not address how these discontinuities affect gradient-based optimization. Please clarify if the training algorithm requires specific smoothing or if subgradient methods are used to handle these points.

comments.title: Ambiguity in activation function domain
comments.quote: In the literature $\sigma$ is acting component-wise on an input vector.
comments.explanation: The paper defines sigma for scalars and then mentions it acts component-wise on vectors. To ensure the composition in Equation (2) is well-defined, please explicitly define the vector-valued extension: 'For a vector v in R^k, we define sigma(v) = (sigma(v_1), ..., sigma(v_k))^T.'

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a trainable matrix activation framework that replaces fixed nonlinearities with learnable, piecewise constant scaling factors. While the core idea of adapting activations to the data is reasonable, the implementation relies on diagonal matrices that function as per-neuron scalars, and the experimental validation lacks modern baselines, computational accounting, and stability analysis. The methodological framing overstates the role of matrix interactions, and the training protocol is too rigid to support the claimed robustness.

The paper tackles a relevant problem by exploring data-adaptive activation functions and provides initial numerical evidence that piecewise constant learnable activations can improve function approximation. The derivation of the diagonal formulation is straightforward, and the CIFAR results show modest accuracy gains over standard ReLU. However, the contribution is weakened by the lack of comparison to contemporary activations, missing computational metrics, and insufficient analysis of training dynamics for discontinuous functions.

Overview issues:

- The proposed matrix activation reduces to a per-neuron piecewise constant function in practice: Section 2 introduces trainable diagonal matrices $D_\ell$ and later proposes tri-diagonal or full activation matrices in Equation 7. All numerical experiments in Section 3 rely exclusively on the diagonal formulation. The core mechanism is simply a learnable, piecewise constant scaling applied independently to each neuron, which closely mirrors existing parametric or spline-based activations. By emphasizing a matrix structure without demonstrating cross-feature interactions, the paper obscures the actual computational and representational gains. Readers will question whether the matrix terminology adds value or merely complicates the notation. To resolve this, the authors should either run ablation studies with the tri-diagonal or sparse full matrices to justify the added complexity, or reframe the contribution as a learnable piecewise constant activation function and compare it directly to modern scalar alternatives.

- Baseline comparisons exclude contemporary learnable and smooth activations: The experimental evaluation in Section 3.3 compares TMAF only against ReLU, Parametric ReLU, and a few older fixed activations. Modern deep learning relies heavily on smooth, learned, or architecture-specific activations such as Swish, SiLU, GELU, and recent piecewise linear units like PWLU or PAU. Without head-to-head comparisons under identical training protocols, the claim that TMAF is more robust remains unsubstantiated. The CIFAR results show marginal gains over ReLU, but these could easily be attributed to the extra parameters or different optimization landscapes rather than the activation form itself. The authors must add a comprehensive baseline table including Swish, GELU, and at least one modern learnable activation, trained with the same optimizer, learning rate schedule, and data augmentations.

- Training discontinuous piecewise constant activations lacks gradient stability analysis: Equation 6 defines $\alpha_\ell$ as a step function, which introduces discontinuities at the grid points $\{s_j\}$. Gradient-based optimizers like Adam struggle with non-differentiable points, often leading to unstable training or vanishing gradients when activations saturate at zero or constant values. The paper fixes the grid points a priori and only trains the step values, but provides no analysis of how gradients flow through these discontinuities or how the network avoids getting stuck in flat regions. This omission is critical because the claimed robustness directly depends on stable joint optimization of weights and activation parameters. The authors should report gradient norms over training epochs, visualize activation statistics, and discuss regularization strategies or smooth approximations to ensure reliable convergence.

- Missing computational overhead analysis and parameter efficiency metrics: Introducing trainable step values for every neuron in every hidden layer significantly increases the parameter count compared to standard ReLU networks. For CIFAR-10 with ResNet18, this could add thousands to millions of parameters depending on the number of intervals $m$. The paper does not report the total parameter count, FLOPs, inference latency, or memory footprint for TMAF models. Without these metrics, it is impossible to assess whether the modest accuracy gains justify the computational cost. A top-tier venue expects a clear trade-off analysis between performance and efficiency. The authors should add a table reporting parameters, FLOPs, and inference time for all architectures, and discuss whether techniques like weight decay or parameter sharing are needed to prevent overfitting.

- Rigid training protocol and underpowered network architectures limit validity: Section 3 applies a fixed learning rate schedule and batch size 500 across all experiments, including MNIST with a 2-layer network of 10 neurons and CIFAR with ResNet18/34. This one-size-fits-all approach is unlikely to yield optimal results for either the tiny FC network or the deeper ResNets. The MNIST experiment uses an unusually small network that cannot capture the representational capacity needed to draw meaningful conclusions about activation functions. Furthermore, the CIFAR experiments reuse hyperparameters from a standard PyTorch tutorial without clarifying whether TMAF required tuning. The authors should use architecture-appropriate optimizers and learning rate schedules, expand the MNIST network to a standard size, and include a hyperparameter sensitivity check or at least state that identical settings were strictly maintained for fair comparison.

- Proposed tri-diagonal and full matrix activations lack empirical or theoretical validation: Equation 7 introduces off-diagonal terms $\beta_\ell$ and $\gamma_\ell$ to create a tri-diagonal activation operator, and the text mentions that full matrix activations are theoretically possible. Section 3 contains zero experiments, ablations, or theoretical discussion regarding these structures. The paper asserts that sparse matrices would be computationally efficient but provides no analysis of how off-diagonal interactions affect gradient flow, expressivity, or training stability. This creates a major gap between the methodological claims and the empirical evidence. The authors should either remove the tri-diagonal/full matrix discussion from the main text or include a dedicated experiment demonstrating its impact, along with a complexity analysis showing how sparsity patterns scale with network width.

- Gradient handling at discontinuities is unspecified: Equation 6 defines the activation as a step function with fixed grid points, which creates non-differentiable boundaries during backpropagation. The paper does not explain how gradients are computed at these breakpoints or how the optimizer avoids stalling in flat regions. This omission is critical because the claimed robustness depends entirely on stable joint optimization of weights and activation parameters. The authors should specify the exact subgradient rule or introduce a differentiable approximation, such as a softmax-weighted combination of step values, and report gradient norms over training epochs to demonstrate that the network does not suffer from vanishing or exploding gradients.

- Robustness claims lack stress-test validation: The abstract and introduction assert that TMAF is more robust than classical adaptive tuning methods like PReLU, but the experiments only report standard classification accuracy on clean datasets. Robustness in deep learning typically requires evaluation under distribution shift, input noise, or adversarial perturbations. The authors should test the proposed activations on corrupted versions of CIFAR-10 or under label noise, and compare the accuracy degradation curves against ReLU and PReLU. Alternatively, they could analyze the conditioning of the layer-wise Jacobians or the sharpness of the loss minima, as these metrics directly relate to the stability claims made in the text.

- Uniform training protocol masks optimization artifacts: Section 3 applies a single learning rate schedule and batch size across all experiments, from a two-layer network on MNIST to ResNet18/34 on CIFAR. This one-size-fits-all approach risks attributing performance gains to the activation function when they may simply stem from a learning rate that happens to suit TMAF better than ReLU. The authors need to include a hyperparameter sensitivity analysis or demonstrate that TMAF converges reliably across a wider range of learning rates. Without showing that the gains persist under varied optimization settings, the reported improvements remain tied to a specific, potentially overfitted training configuration.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add head-to-head comparisons against modern learnable activations (Swish, GELU, PWLU) under identical training protocols and data augmentations.

- Report parameter counts, FLOPs, inference latency, and memory footprint for all architectures to quantify the efficiency trade-offs.

- Include gradient norm tracking, activation statistics, and stability analysis to demonstrate reliable convergence through the discontinuous step functions.

- Either implement and evaluate the tri-diagonal/full matrix variants or reframe the contribution as a per-neuron piecewise constant activation and adjust the theoretical framing accordingly.

- Replace the 10-neuron MNIST setup with a standard-sized fully connected network and use architecture-appropriate learning rate schedules to ensure fair evaluation.

comments.title: Contradiction between matrix multiplication and operation description
comments.quote: The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons.
comments.explanation: This statement is mathematically inconsistent. Matrix-vector multiplication inherently requires summation (dot products), not merely scalar multiplications and comparisons. If the operation is truly limited to element-wise scaling, the authors should replace "matrix-vector multiplications" with "element-wise scaling" to avoid confusion. If cross-feature interactions are intended, the description must explicitly mention the addition operations required for general matrix-vector products.

comments.title: Conflation of dying ReLU and vanishing gradient problems
comments.quote: A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem
comments.explanation: The dying ReLU problem and the vanishing gradient problem are distinct optimization issues. Vanishing gradients historically plagued saturating activations like sigmoid, whereas ReLU was introduced specifically to maintain a constant gradient of 1 for positive inputs. The dying ReLU problem refers only to neurons permanently outputting zero. The authors should rewrite this to accurately distinguish between permanent neuron death and the general vanishing gradient phenomenon.

comments.title: Theoretical expressivity does not guarantee empirical performance
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: Containing ReLU and LeakyReLU as parameterized special cases establishes theoretical expressivity bounds, but it does not ensure that gradient-based optimization will successfully navigate the expanded parameter space to recover those baselines. The introduction of trainable step values significantly increases the non-convexity of the loss landscape, raising risks of poor local minima and initialization sensitivity. The claim should be tempered to reflect that theoretical containment does not guarantee empirical superiority without optimization validation.

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding piecewise constant nature
comments.quote: The activation functions used in TMAF neural network are not piecewise constants. Instead, TMAF activation is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants.
comments.explanation: The opening sentence asserts that TMAF activations are not piecewise constants, but the very next clause defines the matrix entries as trainable piecewise constants. Since matrix-vector multiplication is a linear operation, applying it to a vector of piecewise constant functions yields another piecewise constant vector. The claim is mathematically false and directly contradicts the method's definition. The authors should remove the denial and rephrase to state that TMAF extends scalar piecewise constant activations to a coupled, matrix-valued structure.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss formula has swapped indices and variable reuse
comments.quote: $\mathcal{E}(\mathcal{W},\mathcal{B}) = -\frac{1}{p} \sum_{k=1}^p \log\left(\frac{\exp(x_{c_k,k})}{\sum_{j=1}^M \exp(x_{j,k})}\right).$
comments.explanation: The notation $x_j \in \mathbb{R}^M$ defines the network output for the $j$-th sample, implying the first index denotes the sample and the second denotes the class. In the loss formula, however, the indices are reversed, treating the first index as the class and the second as the sample. Furthermore, the dummy index $j$ in the denominator clashes with the sample index $j$ used earlier. The formula requires consistent indexing to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Section lacks theoretical approximation bounds
comments.quote:
our first example, we use neural networks to approximate

$f(x_1,\cdots,x_n) = \sin(\pi x_1 + \cdots + \pi x_n),\quad
comments.explanation: The section is titled 'Approximation of a smooth function,' which typically implies a theoretical analysis of approximation capability, such as error bounds or convergence rates. However, the provided text contains only experimental setup details and empirical observations. There is no mathematical proof or derivation establishing how the TMAF architecture approximates the target function. The authors should add a brief theoretical remark bounding the approximation error or rename the section to reflect its purely empirical nature.

comments.title: Misplaced tables contradict section focus
comments.explanation: The section explicitly introduces a 1D target function and describes experiments with 3 hidden layers. However, the table reports results for a multivariate function using 1 and 2 hidden layers. This mismatch indicates the results belong to a different experiment and disrupts the logical flow. The authors should remove the table from this section or align the experimental description with the reported results.

comments.title: Undefined parameter in tri-diagonal TMAF interval definition
comments.quote: The tri-diagonal TMAF is given in equation 7, where  $\alpha_{\ell}$  is the same as the diagonal TMAF, while  $\beta_{\ell}$  is piecewise constant with respect to intervals  $(- \infty, -2.01 + \underline{h}]$ ,
comments.explanation: The symbol $\underline{h}$ appears in the interval definition for $\beta_\ell$ but is never defined in the text. Given that $h = 0.02$ was defined earlier for the diagonal TMAF, this is almost certainly a typographical error. The authors should replace $\underline{h}$ with $h$ to maintain consistency and ensure the interval boundaries are clearly specified.

comments.title: Gaps in piecewise interval definitions for off-diagonal terms
comments.quote: while  $\beta_{\ell}$  is piecewise constant with respect to intervals  $(- \infty, -2.01 + \underline{h}]$ ,  $\left\{(-2.01 + kh, -2.01 + (k + 1)h]\right\}_{k=0}^{99}$ ,  $(-0.01, \infty)$
comments.explanation: The intervals as written leave out specific points, failing to partition $\mathbb{R}$. For $\beta_\ell$, the middle intervals end at $-0.03$, and the next interval starts at $-0.01$, leaving the point $-0.01$ undefined. Similarly, for $\gamma_\ell$, the final interval excludes $2.01$. To properly define the activation function on the entire domain, these boundaries must be adjusted to use closed brackets at the transition points.

comments.title: Inconsistent evaluation reporting across datasets
comments.quote: In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: For CIFAR-10, the authors explicitly state that TMAF returns better classification results in the evaluation stage. For CIFAR-100, they only claim outperformance in the training process. Readers will assume TMAF fails to generalize on the larger dataset or that evaluation results were omitted. The authors should clarify whether evaluation accuracy also improves to avoid confusion about the model's generalization performance.

comments.title: Misplaced figure in classification section
comments.quote: Figure 6: Approximations to  $\sin (100\pi x) + \cos (50\pi x) + \sin (\pi x)$ , ReLU-type
comments.explanation: Figure 6 and its caption discuss approximating a trigonometric function, which belongs in a theoretical or introductory analysis section, not in Section 3.3 dedicated to MNIST and CIFAR classification experiments. The figure placement disrupts the flow of the empirical results and confuses the reader about the section's scope. The authors should move this figure to the appropriate theoretical section.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes trainable matrix activation functions (TMAF) as a generalization of ReLU, using piecewise constant step functions whose values are learned during training. The idea is intuitively appealing and the paper demonstrates promising results on synthetic oscillatory function approximation and CIFAR classification. However, the paper suffers from several critical weaknesses: it lacks rigorous theoretical justification, provides insufficient empirical validation (no ablation studies, no statistical significance, limited baselines), and does not adequately address computational cost or training stability. The contribution is not clearly delineated from existing work on learnable activations.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient theoretical justification for expressiveness: The paper claims that TMAF is 'clearly not worse than the traditional ones' because it includes ReLU and Leaky ReLU as special cases, but this is a trivial property shared by many learnable activation functions (e.g., PReLU, PWLU). The paper does not provide any theoretical analysis of the representational power of TMAF compared to standard activations or other learnable ones. For instance, does the piecewise constant structure with trainable values offer any universal approximation advantage over fixed activations with the same number of parameters? The paper mentions that piecewise constant functions can approximate continuous functions arbitrarily well, but this is a standard approximation theory result that applies to any piecewise constant function, not just those learned. The paper needs to either provide a theoretical bound on the approximation error of TMAF networks or clearly state the limitations of the current theoretical understanding.

- Lack of ablation studies isolating the effect of TMAF: The empirical evaluation does not include any ablation studies to isolate the effect of the matrix activation from other architectural choices. For example, in the CIFAR experiments, TMAF is used with ResNet18 and ResNet34, but the paper does not compare against a baseline where the number of parameters is matched by increasing network width or depth. The improvement from 77.5% to 80.2% on CIFAR-10 could be due to the additional parameters from the trainable activation rather than the activation function itself. The paper should include experiments where the baseline network's capacity is increased to match the TMAF network's total parameter count, and also compare against simply adding more layers or wider layers with ReLU.

- Potential overfitting without adequate regularization analysis: The TMAF introduces many additional parameters: for each layer, with m intervals, there are m+1 trainable values per neuron (for diagonal) or per off-diagonal entry (for tri-diagonal). In the oscillatory function example, the diagonal TMAF uses 100 intervals, adding 101 parameters per neuron per layer. With 400 neurons and 3 hidden layers, this adds over 120,000 parameters beyond the weights and biases. The paper does not discuss regularization strategies for these extra parameters, nor does it report validation performance or overfitting metrics. The training loss curves show TMAF achieving lower training loss, but without test/validation accuracy or regularization analysis, it is unclear whether the gains generalize. The paper should include cross-validation results, report test accuracy, and discuss whether techniques like weight decay or dropout are applied to the activation parameters.

- Limited comparison with state-of-the-art activation functions: The paper compares TMAF only against ReLU and Parametric ReLU (PReLU). It does not compare against other modern learnable or adaptive activation functions such as Swish, GELU, ELU, SELU, Pade Activation Units (PAU), Piecewise Linear Units (PWLU), or adaptive splines. Given that the paper claims TMAF is a new approach to trainable activations, it is essential to compare against a broad set of baselines on standard benchmarks. Without such comparisons, it is impossible to assess whether TMAF offers any advantage over existing methods. The paper should include experiments comparing TMAF against at least Swish, GELU, and PWLU on CIFAR-10/100.

- No analysis of computational cost or training stability: The paper does not discuss the computational overhead of TMAF compared to standard activations. The matrix-vector multiplication in TMAF, especially for tri-diagonal or full matrices, introduces additional operations per forward pass. The paper should report training time per epoch, number of floating-point operations, or memory usage for TMAF versus ReLU. Additionally, the paper does not analyze training stability: the piecewise constant activation can introduce discontinuities in the gradient (since the step function has zero gradient almost everywhere), which may cause optimization difficulties. The paper should discuss how gradients are computed through the piecewise constant activation (e.g., using straight-through estimators or surrogate gradients) and whether this affects convergence.

- Missing analysis of learned matrix properties: The paper does not provide any analysis of what the learned activation functions look like after training. For a method that claims to learn activation functions adaptively, it is crucial to visualize the learned piecewise constant functions, examine their eigenvalues or sparsity patterns, and interpret how they differ across layers or tasks. The paper shows only the predefined interval structure but never shows the trained t_{l,j} values. This makes it difficult to understand why TMAF works and whether it is learning meaningful nonlinearities. The paper should include plots of the learned activation functions for different layers and datasets, and discuss any patterns observed (e.g., whether certain intervals are consistently assigned high or low values).

- Assumption that TMAF is universally beneficial without evidence: The paper implicitly assumes that the matrix activation is beneficial across different architectures and tasks, but the evidence is mixed. On MNIST, TMAF achieves only 92.2% accuracy compared to 91.8% for ReLU and 91.5% for PReLU — a tiny improvement that may not be statistically significant.
